title: Toward Foundation Model for Multivariate Wearable Sensing of Physiological Signals

reviews.summary: The paper proposes NORMWEAR, a foundation model for multivariate wearable physiological signals that can operate across arbitrary sensor types and channel counts. The method combines a modality- and number-agnostic CWT-based tokenization pipeline, a shared-weight per-channel transformer encoder with a channel-aware [CLS]-based fusion mechanism, and a masked autoencoding pretraining objective. For zero-shot transfer, a Memory Stream inspired Temporal Fusion (MSiTF) module aligns sensor embeddings with language using frozen encoders and relevance/recency/importance scores. Evaluated on 11 public datasets spanning 18 downstream tasks (state inference, EEG analysis, vital sign estimation, and disease risk), NORMWEAR generally outperforms strong baselines in zero-shot, partial-shot, and full-shot settings.
strengths: The channel-aware attention with liaison [CLS] tokens is a neat fusion mechanism that supports a variable number of input channels, reduces complexity relative to all-token attention, and empirically outperforms cross-attention and mean-pooling variants.
The use of a shared-weight per-channel encoder allows flexible handling of heterogeneous multivariate inputs while maintaining compute efficiency and type-agnostic processing.
The CWT-based multi-scale tokenization over raw and derivative signals provides a unified, modality-agnostic front-end that avoids per-sensor bespoke preprocessing.
The zero-shot MSiTF aggregation module, combining relevance, recency, and importance scoring with frozen encoders, is a thoughtful design tailored to weakly labeled, dynamic human-sensing signals.
Broad and challenging evaluation across 18 tasks and 11 datasets, including EEG, ECG, PPG, GSR, IMU, and PCG; covers zero-shot, partial-shot, and full-shot regimes.
Comparisons against diverse baselines: statistical features, time-series SSL (TF-C), forecasting (Chronos), spectrogram-based (CLAP), and modality-specific FMs (PaPaGei, ECG-FM, CBraMod).
Ablations on masking strategies and fusion mechanisms with complexity discussion; claims of channel-permutation invariance verified empirically.
Clear high-level motivation, pipeline overviews, and design choices; diagrams help convey the architecture and training setup.
The problem setting (arbitrary sensor configurations in wearables) is well-motivated and distinct from generic univariate time-series FMs.
Addresses an important gap: a generalist foundation model for heterogeneous wearable sensing with multivariate, modality-diverse inputs.
Potentially impactful for digital health research and applications where sensor configurations vary across devices, cohorts, and tasks.
weaknesses: Ambiguity around the reconstruction target: inputs are CWT scalograms but the decoder is described as reconstructing “raw physiological signals both temporally and spatially.” If reconstructing waveforms, details on inverse CWT or consistent mapping from scalogram to time-domain are missing; if reconstructing spectrograms/scalograms, this should be clarified.

Handling heterogeneous sampling rates, window lengths, and alignment across modalities is under-specified. Using a fixed Mexican Hat wavelet with scales 1–64 across all modalities raises questions about frequency coverage and comparability across different sampling rates.

The model emphasizes permutation invariance across channels, but many tasks depend on channel identity (e.g., ECG lead types, EEG montages). The absence of explicit sensor-type or channel-position embeddings may limit performance or interpretability in settings where identity matters.
The CWT front-end may impose nontrivial compute and memory overhead. There is little analysis of preprocessing cost or end-to-end latency for real-time wearables.

Heuristic data augmentation expands pretraining by an order of magnitude, but Algorithm 1 and augmentation specifics are not included in the main text; it is difficult to assess potential biases, diversity, and whether augmentations might inadvertently simplify downstream tasks.

Baseline protocol fairness: Chronos and CLAP are not purpose-built for multivariate sensor fusion; concatenating per-channel embeddings may disadvantage them relative to a design that natively fuses channels. Additional recent time-series FMs (e.g., large-scale wearable FMs or multivariate SSL methods) could strengthen comparisons.

Limited statistical reporting: many results lack variance estimates or statistical tests. Some linear-probe accuracies appear unusually high for standard benchmarks (e.g., >98% on UCI-HAR, near-ceiling seizure performance), warranting more rigorous validation and data-split details.

Zero-shot setup depends on text templates and a clinical TinyLlama encoder; the extent of template engineering and sensitivity to prompt wording are not fully discussed. The choice of negatives and potential label-surface overlap across datasets need clarification.
Occasional inconsistencies (e.g., “NORMWEAR” vs “NormWear”), duplicated or fragmented sentences, and formatting artifacts impede precise understanding in some sections.

Some core training details (masking ratios, patch sizes, optimizer/schedules, temperatures, negative sampling) are deferred to the appendix; a brief, self-contained summary in the main text would help.

Prior or concurrent efforts on wearable/time-series foundation models (e.g., Abbaspourazad et al., Narayanswamy et al., broader multivariate SSL literature), audio MAE/BEATs-style self-supervised pretraining, and biosignal-language alignment could be more comprehensively discussed and contrasted.
More discussion of EEG/ECG-specific foundation models and how NORMWEAR trades some modality-optimized performance for generality.
reviews.questions:
1. What exactly is reconstructed during MAE pretraining: the scalogram tokens or the raw time-domain waveforms? If the latter, how is the inverse mapping from CWT patches handled, and what loss is applied?
2. How are different sampling rates and segment lengths across modalities handled before CWT (e.g., resampling strategy, window sizes)? How do scales 1–64 map to comparable frequency bands for different modalities?
3. Please detail the augmentation pipeline used to expand pretraining to 2.5M segments: types of transforms per modality, parameters, and ablations quantifying their effect versus simply adding more unaugmented data.
4. How sensitive is zero-shot performance to template wording and the choice of language encoder? Do you provide canonical prompts and alternatives, and did you measure variance across prompt sets?
5. Do you use any explicit channel-type or sensor-identity embeddings? If not, how does the model disambiguate, for example, ECG leads or EEG montages when identity is crucial? Could adding lightweight type embeddings improve performance?
6. Can you report end-to-end compute: CWT preprocessing time/memory, training FLOPs, and inference latency for typical wearable setups (e.g., 3–6 channels, 10–60 s segments)?
7. How are negatives defined in the contrastive alignment, especially given label overlap across tasks (e.g., related affect states)? What temperature and loss weighting are used for the auxiliary vector-distance term?
8. For baselines like Chronos/CLAP, did you try any stronger multichannel fusion than simple concatenation (e.g., attention-based fusion of per-channel features) to ensure fairness?
9. Are all downstream splits subject-independent where appropriate? Please provide multiple-seed variability or confidence intervals for the main tables.
10. Will you release the pretraining data curation scripts, augmentation code, and the exact text templates used for zero-shot evaluation?
reviews.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and underexplored problem: a general, multivariate, modality-agnostic foundation model for wearable physiological sensing with support for zero-/few-shot transfer. The combination of a CWT-based tokenization pipeline, a share-weight per-channel encoder with [CLS]-based fusion, and a pragmatic sensor-language alignment module is well-motivated and, based on the presented results, effective across a broad suite of healthcare-relevant tasks. The evaluation breadth and consistent gains over diverse baselines are compelling.

That said, several aspects need clarification to solidify the contribution. Chief among them are the reconstruction target and training signal for MAE, handling of heterogeneous sampling rates and calibration of CWT scales across modalities, details and potential biases of the large-scale augmentation, and stronger statistical reporting. Baseline fairness and additional comparisons to recent multivariate/wearable FMs would further strengthen the case. Despite these concerns, the paper’s core ideas are promising, the problem is important, and the empirical results are encouraging. With clarifications and modest extensions, I view this as a valuable contribution to the NeurIPS community and lean toward acceptance.
